coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots originated from Lycii Fructus for effective fluorescent sensing of ferric ion and multicolor cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Xiaohan
He, Jiang
Yang, Shenghong
Zheng, Mingda
Wang, Yingying
Ma, Shuang
Zheng, Haipeng
coredata.dc:description: Green, economical and effective method was developed for synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), using one-pot hydrothermal treatment of Lycii Fructus. Optical and structural properties of the CDs have been extensively studied by UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopic, x-ray diffraction (XRD) techniques, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM). Surface functionality and composition of CDs has been illustrated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra and elemental analysis. The fabricated CDs possess stable fluorescent properties. The fluorescent quantum yield of the CDs can reach 17.2%. The prepared CDs emitted a broad fluorescence between 415 and 545nm and their fluorescence was tuned by changing excitation wavelength. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs could be significantly quenched by Fe3+ (turn-off). The CDs exhibit captivating sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ with a linear range from 0 to 30μM and a detection limit of 21nM. The prepared CDs were successfully applied to the determination of Fe3+ in the urine samples, the water samples from the from the Yellow River and living HeLa (Henrietta Lacks) cells. Moreover, the low-toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of the CDs were evaluated through MTT assay on HeLa cells. The CDs were also employed as fluorescent probes for multicolor imaging of HeLa cells successfully.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Lycii Fructus
Fe3+ sensing
Multicolor cellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytotoxicity Assay and Intracellular Sensing of Fe3+
Cytotoxicity, Multicolor Cellular Imaging and Intracellular Sensing of Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the CDs. (b) the corresponding size distribution histogram of the CDs from DLS. (c) XRD pattern of the CDs and (d) FT-IR spectra of the CDs.
(a) XPS of the CDs and (b) C1s, (c) O1s, and (d) N1s spectra of the CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra (red line) and PL spectra (black line) of the CDs. Inset: photographs of the CDs under daylight (left) and UV light source (right, 365nm). (b) the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs under various excitation wavelengths from 300nm to 500nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Dependence of the PL intensity of the CDs on the pH values from 2 to 12. (b) Stability of the PL intensity with the continuous scan time. (c) The relationship of PL intensity and the different concentration NaCl solution. (d) Stability of the PL intensity with the uninterrupted UV irradiation time.

(a) PL selectivity of the CDs in the presence of various metal ions. (b) PL response of the CDs toward Fe3+ (black bars) and interference of other metal ions with Fe3+ (red bars). (c) PL spectra of the CDs dispersion in the presence of different Fe3+ concentrations. (d) Plot of the PL intensity against Fe3+ concentration within the range of 0–100μM. Inset: the linear relation of (I
0
−
I)/I
0 with concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 0–30μM. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Cell viability of HeLa cells in the presence of different concentrations of the CDs.

Fluorescence microscope images of the CDs under bright-field (a, e), UV-filter (b, f), V-filter (c) and B-filter (d). (e, f) cells were incubated with the CDs and then incubated with Fe3+. (UV-filter, λ=330–400nm; V-filter, λ=395–415nm; B-filter, λ=420–485nm.) (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the preparation of the CDs and their application for Fe3+ detection and multicolor cell imaging.
Comparison of sensing performance of different fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection.
Determination of Fe3+ in samples by proposed method (n =5).

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots originated from Lycii Fructus for effective fluorescent sensing of ferric ion and multicolor cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anqiu People's Hospital, Anqiu 262100, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green, economical and effective method was developed for synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), using one-pot hydrothermal treatment of Lycii Fructus. Optical and structural properties of the CDs have been extensively studied by UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopic, x-ray diffraction (XRD) techniques, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM). Surface functionality and composition of CDs has been illustrated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra and elemental analysis. The fabricated CDs possess stable fluorescent properties. The fluorescent quantum yield of the CDs can reach 17.2%. The prepared CDs emitted a broad fluorescence between 415 and 545nm and their fluorescence was tuned by changing excitation wavelength. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs could be significantly quenched by Fe3+ (turn-off). The CDs exhibit captivating sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ with a linear range from 0 to 30μM and a detection limit of 21nM. The prepared CDs were successfully applied to the determination of Fe3+ in the urine samples, the water samples from the from the Yellow River and living HeLa (Henrietta Lacks) cells. Moreover, the low-toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of the CDs were evaluated through MTT assay on HeLa cells. The CDs were also employed as fluorescent probes for multicolor imaging of HeLa cells successfully.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), as a new kind of nanomaterial in the nanocarbon family, have attracted worldwide attention in recent years. Compared with conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs show distinguished properties such as excellent photostability, strong and tunable luminescence, high aqueous solubility, customizable surface functionalization, low toxicity and favourable biocompatibility, etc. [1–4]. The wide potential application of CDs has been exploited in the fields of sensing [5,6], bio-imaging [7], fluorescent ink [8], photocatalysis [9,10], optoelectronic devices [11] and drug delivery [12]. A variety of approaches for the synthesis of CDs also have been reported, such as hydrothermal method [13], microwave method [14], laser ablation [15], chemical oxidation [16], arc discharge [17] and plasma treatment [18]. However, there are some by-products using conventional chemicals, which do not meet the standard of green chemistry. Therefore, preparation of carbon dots using nontoxic solvents and economical green materials are the key issues that should be taken into consideration in a green synthetic strategy.

Many efforts have been made for the preparation of CDs using eco-friendly chemicals or natural precursors as raw materials. For example, milk [19], honey [20], orange juice [21], pomelo peel [22], Hylocereus undatus [10], banana [23], potato [24], garlic [25], lentinus edodes [26], coffee grounds [27], cotton [28] and even prawn shells [29] have been used as the green sources for CDs. Although these fluorescent CDs have been successfully used as nanoprobes for sensing molecular species and imaging of various cells, the quantum yields (QY) of those CDs mostly are lower than 10%. Hence, it still remains a major challenge to produce biomass-derived CDs with high QY by using simple one-step methodologies.

Lycii Fructus, the dried ripe fruits of Lycium chinense Miller, have long been used as traditional herbal medicine and functional food. The main components of Lycii Fructus include Lycium barbarum polysaccharide, betaine, atropine, gyoscyamine, cerebroside, pyrrole alkaloids and amino acids [30]. These components not only3 afford carbon and nitrogen source for the preparation of CDs, but they might also provide plenty of reactive sites for potential applications in biosensing.

Ferric ion (Fe3+) is one of the most indispensable and abundant metal cations in natural environment, it plays a crucial role in several physiological and pathological processes including cellular metabolism, enzyme catalysis, oxygen transport and RNA synthesis [31,32]. Therefore, the development of effective sensing systems for determination of Fe3+ is great significance for clinical, medical and environmental concerns. Currently, there exist various techniques for quantification of Fe3+ including atomic absorption spectrometry [33], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [34], spectrophotometry [35] and electrochemistry [36]. However, most of these methods require sophisticated and expensive instrumentation and laborious sample treatment procedures. In contrast, fluorescent sensors offer a simple, low-cost and high sensitive approach for assaying metal ions in environmental and biological samples.

Herein, we report a green approach for the preparation of CDs by using Lycii Fructus as raw material through one-pot hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs show well water-soluble and excellent biocompatibility and the QY can reach up to 17.2%. The as-prepared CDs can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ based on the fluorescence quenching. The feasibility of the analysis of Fe3+ by using the CDs was also demonstrated in samples. Moreover, the CDs were employed as fluorescent probes for multicolor cell imaging and intracellular sensing of Fe3+, exhibiting their potential in biomedical fields (Scheme 1 ).

Lycii Fructus was procured from the Xixia District, Yinchuan, Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region. Ammonia solution (25%) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate was purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without any further purification. Ultrapure water used throughout the experiment was supplied by the Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore Corporation, Bedford MA) and resistivity was 18.25MΩ·cm.

Lycii Fructus was used as a carbon source for the preparation of water dispersible CDs via hydrothermal treatment. Briefly, 1g of Lycii Fructus was grinded and mixed with 30mL of ultrapure water. The 30mL of solution was filtered through the vacuum to obtain a pulpless product. Then, the above solution and 1mL ammonia solution (25%) were transferred to a Teflon-lined pressure vessel of 50mL capacity and heated at 200°C for 5h. The product was centrifuged (15,000rpm, 20min), and the resultant supernatant was further purified in H2O using a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off=500) for 12h to obtain the brown-yellow solution of the CDs. The yellow powder of the CDs was obtained through vacuum drying at 60°C.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by an F97Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Lengguang, Shanghai, China). Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were analyzed by UV2800SPC UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Hengping Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained by FEI Tecnai G2F30 (FEI Company, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was observed using a NEXUS 670 (Nicolet, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded using an X' Pert PRO diffractometer (Panalytical, Netherlands). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured by a PHI-5720 multifunctional spectrometer (Spectra-Physics Inc. USA) using AI K radiation. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was measured by BI-200SM (Brookhaven, USA). Cell imaging was observed using a research-grade upright fluorescence microscope, BX53 (Olympus, Japan).

In a typical assay, the CDs powder was dispersed in H2O as stock solution (25μg/mL), and then 3mL stock solution of CDs was added in a quartz cuvette, followed by the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+. After 3min, the PL emission spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 350nm at room temperature. The selectivity for Fe3+ was confirmed by adding other metal ions solutions instead of Fe3+ in a similar way.

The urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers. The samples were diluted 1000-fold with water before analysis. No sample pretreatments such as deproteinization or centrifugation were required. The Yellow River water obtained from the Yellow River in Lanzhou, Gansu province, China. The water samples were filtered through a 0.22μm membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity Assay and Intracellular Sensing of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were cultured in a 1640 medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum supplemented with 100units/mL penicillin and 100mg/mL streptomycin in an incubator at 37°C with 5% CO2. The cell viability was measured using the MTT assay. For cell imaging studies, the HeLa cells were seeded on a coverslip in a 12-well plate 24h before use. The dispersion of the CDs was prepared in 1640 medium with a concentration of 25μg/mL and the cells were incubated with the dispersion of the CDs in culture medium for 2h at 37°C. After the cells were washed three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS), the fluorescent images were acquired. Then, 40μM Fe3+ was added and incubated for 10min. The cells were imaged again after washing the culture medium three times with PBS.

The size distribution and morphology of the obtained CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, the CDs are near spherical morphology and monodispersity nanoparticles, which have a relative narrow size distribution ranging from 2 to 5nm. The size and mono-dispersity of the CDs are further evidenced by the DLS measurements (Fig. 1b), the average diameter is 3.3nm, which is consistent with the results of TEM. From Fig. 1c, one evident peak at 2θ =20° is observed in the XRD of the CDs, which suggests the amorphous nature of CDs [37]. The FT-IR is applied to explore the surface groups of the CDs. As revealed in Fig. 1d, the absorption bands of OH and NH stretching vibrations appears at 3391cm−1. The peak at 2931cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CH. CO and CC stretching vibrations are at 1626cm−1. The peak at 1403cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CN.

The surface composition of the CDs was further confirmed by XPS (Fig. 2 ). The spectra of the CDs show three typical peaks of C1s (282eV), N1s (397eV) and O1s (529eV). Elemental analysis reveals the composition of the CDs to be C (46.4wt%), N (8.4wt%) and O (39.4wt%). The C1s high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 2b) reveal three peaks at 282.1, 283.3 and 285.3eV, corresponding to CC, CN and CO groups, respectively. The O1s spectra (Fig. 2c) display two peaks at 248.4eV and 529.6eV, which can be attributed to CO and C-OH/C-O-C groups. The N1s spectra (Fig. 2d) exhibit two main bands at 396.8eV and 398.0eV, confirming O=CN and CN, respectively [38–40]. All above results indicate that the surface of the CDs is passivated by multiple oxygenated and nitrous groups.

UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the CDs are illustrated in Fig. 3a. The CDs exhibit a strong absorption peak at 271nm together with a shoulder around 300nm. The peak at 271nm is ascribed to the π-π* transition of CN or CC bonds, whereas the peak at 300nm is possibly caused by the n-π* transition of the carbonyl or amine groups on the surface of the CDs [41–43]. The prepared CDs solution is yellowish in color under normal light and exhibits intense blue luminescence under UV excitation (λ=365nm) (inset Fig. 3a). A maximum PL emission is obtained at an emission wavelength of 430nm with excitation wavelength at 350nm. When the excitation wavelength is changed from 300nm to 500nm, the maximum emission peak position shifts to longer wavelength, from 415nm to 545nm, and the PL intensity decreases remarkably. The fluorescent QY of the CDs is measured to be 17.2% (quinine sulphate as a reference), which is higher than other CDs derived from green method [19,22,24,25,26,28,29].

In order to assess the stability of CDs, various experiments were performed. The PL intensities of the CDs at different pH values are evaluated (Fig. 4a). It can be observed that the PL of the CDs is strong and stable in the pH range 3–11, indicating that the CDs can be employed in both acidic and basic environments. When the sample was scanned for 6h continuously, the PL intensity of the CDs barely changed (Fig. 4b). The influence of ionic strength on the fluorescent behavior of the CDs is evaluated as depicted in Fig. 4c. There is no obvious change of PL intensity can be observed at a NaCl concentration of up to 3.0M, suggesting high PL stability of the CDs under high ionic strength conditions. As found in Fig. 4d, less than 6% decline in emission intensity was detected even when the CDs were irradiated for 7h with UV light continuously. In general, these results indicate excellent stability of the CDs, making them promising for practical application in metal ions detection and bioimaging.

Taken together, the CDs satisfy the requirements of fluorescent probe. For selectivity study of the CDs toward different metal ions, various metal ions were added into the CDs aqueous solution, including K+, Li+, Na+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Al3+ and Fe3+, respectively, at the same concentration of 40μM. The change ratio (I
0
− I)/I
0 of PL was displayed in Fig. 5a (here I and I
0 are the PL intensities of the CDs at 430nm in the presence and absence of iron ions). Fe3+ had the ability to quench the PL intensity of the CDs while other metal ions had a little or no quenching effects. The selectivity toward Fe3+ was also investigated in the presence of other metal ions, as shown in Fig. 5b. No obvious PL intensity change was observed after other metal ions were added. Then, addition of Fe3+ into the above mixtures caused a sharp decrease in PL intensity, which demonstrated that other metal ions showd no interference toward the determination of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. S1, the absorbance intensity is increased with the increase of Fe3+ concentrations. It suggests that the inner filter effect (IFE) may be one of the reasons for the fluorescence quenching of CDs induced by Fe3+ [44]. Furthermore, according to the XPS results and FTIR data, there were plentiful the C-OH/C-O-C groups on the surface of the CDs. The hydroxyl groups on the CDs could react with Fe3+ and form complexes due to coordination [45–47]. The complexes may accelerate the nonradiative electron/hole recombination through effective photoelectron or energy transfer, further results in the fluorescence quenching. Above results strongly indicated that the CDs have excellent selectivity toward Fe3+ sensing. The exact origin of the selectivity of the CDs need more experimental evidence, but the coordination of hydroxyl groups with Fe3+ and the unfilled orbit of Fe3+ should be the most possible reasons.

Taken together, the CDs satisfy the requirements of fluorescent probe. For selectivity study of the CDs toward different metal ions, various metal ions were added into the CDs aqueous solution, including K+, Li+, Na+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Al3+ and Fe3+, respectively, at the same concentration of 40μM. The change ratio (I
0
− I)/I
0 of PL was displayed in Fig. 5a (here I and I
0 are the PL intensities of the CDs at 430nm in the presence and absence of iron ions). Fe3+ had the ability to quench the PL intensity of the CDs while other metal ions had a little or no quenching effects. The selectivity toward Fe3+ was also investigated in the presence of other metal ions, as shown in Fig. 5b. No obvious PL intensity change was observed after other metal ions were added. Then, addition of Fe3+ into the above mixtures caused a sharp decrease in PL intensity, which demonstrated that other metal ions showd no interference toward the determination of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. S1, the absorbance intensity is increased with the increase of Fe3+ concentrations. It suggests that the inner filter effect (IFE) may be one of the reasons for the fluorescence quenching of CDs induced by Fe3+ [44]. Furthermore, according to the XPS results and FTIR data, there were plentiful the C-OH/C-O-C groups on the surface of the CDs. The hydroxyl groups on the CDs could react with Fe3+ and form complexes due to coordination [45–47]. The complexes may accelerate the nonradiative electron/hole recombination through effective photoelectron or energy transfer, further results in the fluorescence quenching. Above results strongly indicated that the CDs have excellent selectivity toward Fe3+ sensing. The exact origin of the selectivity of the CDs need more experimental evidence, but the coordination of hydroxyl groups with Fe3+ and the unfilled orbit of Fe3+ should be the most possible reasons.

Besides selectivity, their sensitivity was also investigated. Upon addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 0–100μM, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs solution was gradually quenched (Fig. 5c). The results shown in Fig. 5d indicate that the ratios of (I
0
− I)/I
0 had a good linear correlation with the Fe3+ concentrations in the range of 0–30μM. The detection limit (DL) was estimated to be 21nM (DL=3SD/S, where “SD” is the standard error of the blank, “S” is the slope of the calibration curve), which was still lower than the maximum level (5mM) of Fe3+ in drinking water permitted by the EPA [48]. The comparison between the proposed method and the earlier reported methods is listed in Table 1 . The superior sensitivity is much better than those previous reported probes. All these observations indicate that the CDs fluorescent sensing probe exhibits very promising application toward the Fe3+ detection.

The feasibility of the CDs for detecting Fe3+ was validated in water samples from the Yellow River and urine samples. As listed in Table 2 , the recoveries of the Yellow River water samples and urine samples were 97.4%–103.1% and 96.8%–102.5%, respectively. In addition, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of Fe3+ was in the range of 0.86%–1.14%. These results suggest that the CDs are capable of detecting Fe3+ ions in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity, Multicolor Cellular Imaging and Intracellular Sensing of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The toxicity of nanomaterials is crucial for their biomedical applications, thus the toxicity of the CDs was studied by using HeLa cell lines. As expected, the cell viability was estimated to be greater than 90% even though the concentration of the CDs was increased to 200μg/mL (Fig. 6 ). High cell viability confirmed the excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity and the great potential of the CDs for monitoring Fe3+ in living cells.

To demonstrate the potential application of CDs as a bioimaging probe, in vitro cellular uptake experiments in HeLa cells were performed. HeLa cells were tagged with the prepared CDs and the images were taken under upright fluorescence microscope. As shown in Fig. 7a–d, bright blue, glaucous and green luminescence were observed in living cells under the UV-filter, V-filter, and B-filter, respectively. After addition of Fe3+ to cells labelled with the CDs, the PL intensity decreased obviously (Fig. 7f). All these results demonstrate that the CDs are membrane permeable and could be used to multicolor cellular imaging and visualize the Fe3+ in living cells.

In summary, the Lycii Fructus, as the precursor, has been successfully used as a green and economic strategy for producing fluorescent CDs via one-step hydrothermal method. Additionally, the CDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity and were used as fluorescent probes to detect Fe3+ with the detection limit of 21nM. The applications of the CDs for the detection of Fe3+ in the samples were also achieved successfully. Meanwhile, the CDs possessed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, were successfully applied for multicolor cell imaging and intracellular sensing of Fe3+. The experimental results were satisfactory. Therefore, the prepared CDs are promising as an eco-friendly material for in vitro or in vivo multicolor imaging and sensing applications.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Supplementary Fig S1
UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ (from bottom to top, 0, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100μM).
Supplementary Fig S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2017.08.035.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
